identity.title: Bolt, Nut, Screw, Rivet, and Washer Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Independent lower-middle-market manufacturers that repeatedly produce customer-specific, private-label, or qualified metal bolts, nuts, screws, rivets, washers, and other industrial fasteners for external OEM and industrial customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Manufacturers selling only standardized proprietary or commodity catalog fasteners, captive plants, precision turned product firms classified elsewhere, plastics fastener producers, pure distributors, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: OEM and industrial customers buy repeat fastener runs under drawings or standards covering material, dimensions, strength, heat treatment, coating, traceability, testing, packaging, and delivery through purchase orders or supply agreements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code includes both commodity catalog fasteners and customer-specific or qualified contract programs. The lens retains recurring outsourced production so eligibility and commercial retention describe one coherent model.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for all fabricated-metal manufacturing rather than eligible 332722 contract fastener producers, and ILO reports global GenAI exposure by broad occupation. I assign higher exposure to quoting, specification review, purchasing, scheduling, quality documentation, inspection-data analysis, customer service, and administration and lower exposure to heading, threading, forming, heat treatment, coating, inspection execution, packaging, and material handling. The broad BLS mix may overstate machining and welding but correctly indicates production-heavy work; GenAI-only evidence may understate machine-vision potential.
a.rationale: Fastener production uses high-speed physical equipment and often already automates feeding and forming. Remaining AI opportunity is concentrated in RFQs, planning, quality and traceability records, maintenance analysis, inspection review, and customer coordination rather than direct machine cycles.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from eligible fastener plants, excluding already automated cycles and measuring actual time in commercial, engineering, setup, production, quality, and administrative workflows, would materially change the range.
caveats: BLS occupation evidence covers NAICS 332 rather than 332722 or the retained contract subset.
ILO exposure is technical potential rather than observed substitution.
Existing heading, threading, feeding, and sorting automation is outside the remaining opportunity.

rho.rationale: Quoting, scheduling, procurement, document control, quality analytics, and maintenance triage can be implemented within five years. Legacy machines, data fragmentation, cybersecurity, product-liability risk, customer qualification, and the need to validate strength and traceability constrain deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Plant pilots measuring quote time, schedule adherence, setup reduction, tool life, scrap, sorting false calls, traceability errors, accepted recommendations, and integration cost would materially change implementation.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in eligible fastener manufacturers.
Digital readiness differs between modern high-volume cold-heading lines and older specialty plants.

e.rationale: Census defines the code by the products and machines, not by a service model, though the broader turned-product and fastener group includes customized machinery and equipment parts. Customer-specific and qualified OEM fasteners can fit the lens, while standardized catalog and commodity production does not.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm review of the 155 dataset firms using customer drawings, private-label and qualified-program revenue, catalog versus contract mix, captive status, repeat orders, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
caveats: The provided firm count is margin-bridged and estimated before applying the outsourced-service lens.
Producing to an industry fastener standard does not itself establish outsourced customer-specific manufacturing.

s5.bridge: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries were age 55 or older, not qualifying control transfers among eligible fastener producers. I use this only as succession pressure and reduce it for the five-year window, internal succession, non-sale exits, institutional or multi-owner firms, equipment debt, customer qualification, concentration, and nontransferable operations. The broad proxy may overstate sale probability for capital-intensive commodity plants but understate succession pressure in founder-led specialty producers.
s5.rationale: Founder succession can generate potential sellers, but completed control transfers require diversified qualified programs, capable managers, maintained equipment, effective quality systems, and reduced owner dependence.
s5.change_evidence: Closed fastener and industrial-component deal records, UCC and ownership changes, and interviews with sector brokers, owners, lenders, and quality registrars over the prior five years would materially change the probability.
caveats: Owner-age data are cross-industry, based on 2018, and count responding owners rather than firms.
Age and succession pressure do not establish sale intent or transaction completion.

q.rationale: Qualification, traceability, tooling, delivery reliability, and switching cost can preserve benefits in critical or custom programs. Competitive bids, distributor leverage, should-cost models, steel surcharges, annual cost-downs, and abundant standardized alternatives pass visible productivity gains to customers.
q.change_evidence: A contract, RFQ, and purchase-order sample showing term, rebid cadence, cost-down clauses, metal pass-through, open-book provisions, tooling ownership, productivity sharing, and historical concessions would materially change retention.
caveats: No public contract dataset measures benefit sharing for eligible fastener producers.
Retention can be much higher for qualified aerospace or medical fasteners than for standardized industrial products.

d5.bridge: BLS real sectoral output and Federal Reserve industrial production measure the broader NAICS 3327 group, not five-year constant-price demand for eligible custom or qualified fastener programs. Real output fell from 102.578 in 2022 to 84.601 in 2025, while industrial production fell from 103.5785 in 2023 to 94.3232 in 2025. I translate this weakness into modest base contraction with recovery upside. The broader series may overstate weakness or strength depending on machine-shop and precision-turning cycles and does not isolate fastener imports.
d5.rationale: Fasteners remain essential physical inputs to vehicles, aerospace, machinery, construction, appliances, infrastructure, and industrial maintenance. Demand is broad but cyclical and exposed to imports, inventory corrections, material substitution, vehicle and machinery builds, and customer consolidation.
d5.change_evidence: Customer build forecasts, bookings, backlog, current import penetration, awarded-program life, maintenance demand, and product-level shipment histories from eligible firms would materially change the ratio.
caveats: The output series combines machine shops, turned products, and fasteners and includes firms outside the lens.
Historical output is not a direct five-year forecast.
Commodity construction fasteners and qualified aerospace fasteners have very different demand patterns.

o.rationale: Remaining demand requires physical heading, threading, forming, heat treatment, coating, sorting, testing, packaging, and accountable conformance. Software can improve planning and quality analysis but cannot supply the fastener; displacement is mainly customer insourcing, supplier consolidation, imports, joining-method substitution, or product redesign.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy plans, supplier-consolidation data, adoption of adhesives or integral joining methods, additive-manufacturing qualifications, and evidence of fully autonomous approved production would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Operator requirement can persist while production shifts offshore or to larger suppliers.
Demand elimination and import substitution are reflected primarily in d5.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is the subset of fastener manufacturers serving recurring custom, private-label, or qualified OEM programs. Labor intensity is meaningful, but production is physical and often already highly automated, while standardized products face severe price and import competition.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve RFQ and specification review, quoting, scheduling, purchasing, tool-life analysis, inspection review, traceability documentation, maintenance triage, and customer service. Heading, threading, forming, heat treatment, coating, sorting, and packaging remain equipment-dependent.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification, traceability, tooling, and reliable delivery can protect savings in critical programs. Standardized alternatives, annual bids, cost-down clauses, should-cost models, distributor power, and metal indices make visible productivity gains vulnerable to pass-through.
narrative.firm_availability: Only a subset of producers meets the outsourced-service lens because many sell standardized catalog fasteners. Founder succession can create transactions, but transferability requires diversified qualified programs, maintained machines, quality certifications, workforce depth, and limited owner dependence.
narrative.demand_durability: Fasteners remain indispensable across most physical industries, but domestic volume is cyclical and exposed to imports, customer inventory, product redesign, and build rates. Qualified critical fasteners should be more durable than commodity bolts and washers.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The case weakens if a target competes primarily on standardized price, relies on one OEM, has obsolete equipment or weak traceability, needs capital automation rather than AI, or accepts mandatory annual cost-downs. Quality escapes and product liability can overwhelm modest labor savings.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring qualified fastener programs create sticky physical demand and improvable commercial, planning, and quality workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Commodity competition, pass-through pressure, and already automated production limit incremental AI value.

sources.title: NAICS Definition: Turned Product and Fastener Manufacturing
sources.supports: Census defines 332722 as manufacturing metal bolts, nuts, screws, rivets, washers, and other industrial fasteners using headers, threaders, and nut-forming machines; the broader 33272 definition includes customized machinery and equipment parts.

sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332
sources.supports: BLS reports 2025 fabricated-metal employment of 56,060 press operators, 67,090 production supervisors, 101,640 machinists, 125,250 team assemblers, and 112,520 welders.

sources.title: Generative AI and Jobs: Policies to Manage the Transition
sources.supports: ILO reports 24% of clerical tasks at high automation exposure and 58% at medium exposure, while other occupational groups have only 1% to 4% highly exposed and no more than 25% medium-exposed tasks.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: Census reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, based on the 2019 Annual Business Survey for data year 2018.

sources.title: Real Sectoral Output: Machine Shops, Turned Product, and Fasteners
sources.supports: BLS real sectoral output for NAICS 3327 declined from an index of 102.578 in 2022 to 84.601 in 2025, with 2017 equal to 100.

sources.title: Metal and Plastic Machine Workers
sources.supports: BLS projects metal and plastic machine-worker employment to decline 7% from 2024 to 2034 and states that firms continue expanding CNC tools and robots to improve quality and lower costs.
